questions.description: "The doctrine of ultra views is redundant, protecting neither shareholders nor creditors and should be abolished." You…
questions.text: "The doctrine of ultra views is redundant, protecting neither shareholders nor creditors and should be abolished." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "Disregarding separate legal personality of related companies by treating them as a single economic entity has…
questions.text: "Disregarding separate legal personality of related companies by treating them as a single economic entity has generally not found favour in the Irish courts". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Ruby runs a wine and port distribution business in Dublin. Company, Director.
questions.text: Ruby runs a wine and port distribution business in Dublin. He comes to you for advice telling you that he is owed some €50,000 from a company called Ever Optimistic Builders Limited, in respect of drink supplied to it before Christmas for which Ruby has not been paid. Ruby tells you that all contact with Ever Optimistic Builders Limited has been futile and it is clear to him that the company has no intention of paying him the money it owes. Ruby tells you that this company has been slow in paying him on a number of occasions in the past and that it was against his better judgment to supply the wine before Christmas as he dislikes and distrusts Fred, the managing director of the company. Ruby acknowledges to you that he owes Ever Optimistic Builders Limited €30,000 in respect of an extension which was carried out to his home but that he has no intention of paying that until his bill is first discharged. Ruby asks you to explain the steps involved in bringing a petition to wind up Ever Optimistic Builders Limited. You are required to advise Ruby on the law relating to presenting a petition to have a company wound up on the grounds that it is unable to pay its debts. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law and attempt to apply these to the facts provided above.

questions.description: "The cornerstone of corporate governance is Regulation 80 of Part I of Table A".
questions.text: "The cornerstone of corporate governance is Regulation 80 of Part I of Table A". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Big Bank plc (the "Bank") is your client and seeks your advice in relation… Company.
questions.text: Big Bank plc (the "Bank") is your client and seeks your advice in relation to security it proposes to take in support of a loan to a company called Can-Do Limited (the "Company"). The Company is seeking to borrow €5,000,000 to finance construction works it has been contracted to carry out. The security which has been offered is as follows: (a) a first fixed charge over the Company's book debts due under the contract and over a bank account into which the Company proposes to pay 75% of each of the five €1m instalments due on foot of the contract; and (b) a floating charge over its plants and machinery. You are required to advise the Bank on the law applicable to each of the two proposed securities so as to ensure that they will be valid and enforceable. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Charlie is a director of Rainy Days Limited (the "Company") and holds 10%… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Charlie is a director of Rainy Days Limited (the "Company") and holds 10% of its issued share capital. Charlie was given his 10% of the shares in the Company by his two fellow shareholding directors as a reward for his many years of service with the Company. The Company is in the printing business and Charlie is the chief typesetter who looks after the printing. Charlie's fellow directors, Richard and Martin, hold between them the remaining 90% of the issued shares in the Company. At the prompting of his brother-in-law, in whom Charlie has been confiding, Charlie comes to you for advice after learning that his fellow directors have recently transferred a significant asset out of the company for what seems to be its market value. Charlie tells you that the Company never really had formal board meetings and that things were no different on this occasion, Richard and Martin simply taking it upon themselves to give effect to the transfer. Charlie has explained that any time he has objected to this course of action, Richard and Martin have reminded him that once they agree to a course of action, there is little point in having a board meeting since the board acts by majority and he would be outvoted; they also remind him that as they hold 90% of the shares they can remove him as a director if they see ft so he has little reason for complaint. You are required to advise Charlie on the law relating to minority shareholder oppression and the remedies available to a member of a company and say whether in your opinion Charlie has a case against the Company or its directors. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "It is now well recognised that a company's members can give their sanction to a course of action in either of three…
questions.text: "It is now well recognised that a company's members can give their sanction to a course of action in either of three ways: first, by a resolution passed at a general meeting of the company, duly convened and held; secondly, by unanimous written resolution and thirdly, by an estoppel, created through their unanimous informal agreement." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Fast Repairs Limited (the "Company") is a family company of which Jim and… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Fast Repairs Limited (the "Company") is a family company of which Jim and Lucy are its directors. Jim and Lucy have each got 20% of the shares in the company as have their three siblings, Lou, Sue and Flo. Although Lucy is a director, she does not play an active part in the management of the Company and is there to fill the statutory requirement that every company must have two directors. Jim is the managing director and Lucy has delegated the full power to manage the Company to Jim. Every year the Company declares a dividend for its five shareholders and in addition to his share of the dividend, Jim also received a salary for working in the Company. Once a year the family get together for a briefing on the Company given by Jim and at that meeting the accounts for the previous year are signed and the annual return completed. The Company's business is that of shoe repairs and business has never been better. Turnover has doubled in the last 12 months and three additional employees have had to be taken on to cater for demand. New premises are required and Jim proposes to rent an adjacent premises (which he owns personally) to the Company to enable it to expand its business; Jim is proposing a lump sum of €25,000 in key money to be paid to him and an annual rent of €30,000 thereafter which Jim believes reflects the market value of his premises. Jim also proposes making a loan to the Company to enable it to fund the key money and intends to charge the Company an interest rate of 5% which, again, Jim believes to be reasonable. Jim is a conscientious sort and approaches you for advice on the formalities which he should observe in putting these transactions into effect. You are required to advise Jim in relation to the duties owed by a director in these circumstances and as to how Jim should go about discharging those duties. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "A person may find himself the subject of the duties and liabilities of a director of an Irish company in either of…
questions.text: "A person may find himself the subject of the duties and liabilities of a director of an Irish company in either of three ways -- by formal appointment as a director, or by finding that he is a de facto director or a shadow director." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "The authority to bind a company can arise through having ostensible authority as well as having express authority, and…
questions.text: "The authority to bind a company can arise through having ostensible authority as well as having express authority, and third parties seeking to enforce contracts may also rely upon the Rule in Turquand's Case and SI 163 of 1973." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case ad statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Leonard and Larry are directors of LL Builders Limited (the "Company") in… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Leonard and Larry are directors of LL Builders Limited (the "Company") in which they are equal 50:50 shareholders. The Company's business has been the purchase, development and resale of period houses. The Company has operated very successfully for the last 10 years; Leonard taking responsibility for the purchase and sale of the properties and Larry looking after the development and building works. Over the last five years Leonard had told Larry that he had put together a portfolio of properties for the Company and that he had been purchasing suitable properties as they came to market so as to ensure a steady supply of properties was available for development by Larry. Larry comes to you for advice. He tells you that he has discovered that Leonard had been acquiring properties not only for the Company but for another company owned by Leonard's son, Frederick. Larry suspects that the better properties (acquired more cheaply with greatest potential for a higher resale value) were siphoned off to Frederick's company. Larry tells you that the Company has three properties on its books now which are worth about 25% less than what the Company paid for them a couple of years ago. Larry believes he has identified two other properties which were destined to be acquired by the Company but instead were diverted to Frederick's company. However, Larry has no reason to suspect that these properties too are now worth less than Frederick paid for them. Larry also tells you that he believes that one derelict property which was acquired by the Company was subsequently sold to Frederick's company for the same price as it paid for it. Larry suspects this property is now worth less than the Company paid for it. You are required to advise Larry of his remedies and those of the Company against Leonard. In your answer you are required to refer to all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "Capital maintenance is a central feature of company law, the purpose of which is designed to protect a company's…
questions.text: "Capital maintenance is a central feature of company law, the purpose of which is designed to protect a company's creditors." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in… Regulation, Company.
questions.text: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the "Company"), which was established by their late mother Madge, having acquired their shares under her will. The Company is in the business of retailing carpet and its only two directors are David and Deborah who also work in the Company. Cindy left the country a number of years ago and lives with her husband in San Francisco and over the years has not had any involvement in the Company. Once a year she contacts her brother and sister to ask how the Company is doing and usually, following that call, David and Deborah declare a dividend which the Company pays to Cindy, averaging over €50,000 per annum for the last three years. Last Christmas when Cindy was home in Ireland she tentatively enquired from David and Deborah whether they would be interested in buying her shares in the Company. They said they would have to think about it because it was hard to know what the shares were worth, given the economic downturn. David and Deborah come to you for advice when Cindy contacted them again and announced that she has sold her shares in the Company to Joe, a local businessman in the same town as where the Company has its premises. A couple of days later David and Deborah were approached by Joe who gave them a stock transfer signed by Cindy and asked them to register him as a member. David and Deborah are unhappy to share the family business with an outsider and ask you for your advice. On examination of the Company's articles of association, you see that Regulation 3 of Part II of Table A applies: "The directors may, in their absolute discretion, and without assigning any reason therefore, decline to register any transfer of any share, whether or not it is a fully paid share". You are required to advise David and Deborah on their rights and obligations in relation to the transfer of shares to Joe. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Niamh and Ruth are directors of and equal 50:50 shareholders in Story Book… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Niamh and Ruth are directors of and equal 50:50 shareholders in Story Book Sales Limited (the "Company") which is in the business of visiting offices and leaving samples of books with the people, and then coming back to take their orders to buy the books. Business has been reasonably good over the years, but recently sales have not been as good. Niamh is shocked to be told by Ruth that they have not paid a number of their creditors for some time. The revenue commissioners have not been paid VAT for two months, their suppliers of the books have also not been paid, and whilst they have continued to collect money from the people in the offices they may not be able to get the books they ordered because their supplier has refused to provide any more books to meet the orders. When Niamh asks to see the books of the company Ruth confesses that she has not been keeping proper books and that the Company's records are "a bit of a mess". Not only have annual returns not been made to the Companies Registration Office, but many of the Company's records and receipts are usually kept by Ruth in the book of her car! Realising that the company is insolvent, and at Niamh's urging, Ruth eventually agrees to put the Company into creditors' voluntary liquidation and a liquidator is appointed. Niamh approaches you for advice on the likelihood that she will be restricted pursuant to section 150 of the Companies Act 1990. You are required to advise Niamh on the law relating to the [restriction of directors]{.underline} and to cite all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "The floating charge, a securing unique to companies, has a number of key…
questions.text: "The floating charge, a securing unique to companies, has a number of key characteristics which distinguish it from a fixed charge." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Trader-Upper Brokers Limited (the "Company") is a mortgage broker company… Mortgage, Company.
questions.text: Trader-Upper Brokers Limited (the "Company") is a mortgage broker company of which Fred and Richard are the only two directors and equal shareholders. The Company is finding the going tough as it specialised in securing mortgage finance from banks and building societies for upwardly-mobile people interested in "trading-up" their homes to bigger and better residences. The Company's finances have been severely affected by the recession: finance has not been available to people to buy houses; houses are not selling and where the Company introduced business to the banks and building societies their commission was less than it had previously been. Frank and Richard have not reduced their salaries of €15,000 per month even though the company's income has fallen dramatically. They continue to incur debts to other creditors and to trade. They approach you for advice when their auditor warned them that to continue as they are could result in their being found to have traded fraudulently or recklessly. You are required to advise Frank and Richard on the law relating to [reckless trading]{.underline} and [fraudulent trading]{.underline}. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.
